critique.summary: The article provides detailed documentation on how to use 'channels' in Claude Code, a feature that allows pushing events into a running Claude Code session from external platforms like Telegram and Discord. It explains setup steps, security measures, enterprise controls, and the research preview status of channels.
mainPoints: Channels enable pushing events into a running Claude Code session, allowing Claude to react to external events.
Supported channels currently include Telegram and Discord, with setup instructions for creating bots and configuring plugins.
Channels require Claude Code version 2.1.80 or later and a claude.ai login; API key authentication is not supported.
Channels are in research preview and require explicit enabling by organization admins for Team and Enterprise users.
Security is managed via sender allowlists and pairing codes to restrict who can send messages through channels.
Users can also test with a fakechat demo channel that runs locally without external authentication.
Enterprise controls allow admins to enable or disable channels for their organizations.
Channels only operate while the Claude Code session is open and require running Claude in a background or persistent terminal for always-on use.
critique.truthfulness: The article appears to be an official documentation page from Claude Code, providing technical instructions and feature descriptions consistent with typical software documentation. The claims about supported channels, setup steps, security, and enterprise controls align with standard practices for integrating chatbots and event-driven plugins. There is no indication of factual inaccuracies, and the information matches publicly available details about Claude Code and its plugin architecture.
considerations: The article does not discuss potential privacy implications of using channels with external platforms like Telegram and Discord.
It does not mention any limitations or performance considerations when running channels in production environments.
The security section could elaborate more on risks related to token management and potential misuse if tokens are leaked.
There is no discussion of alternative methods or competitors offering similar event-pushing capabilities.
The research preview status implies that features and APIs may change, which users should consider before deploying in critical systems.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion centers around the new channels feature in Claude Code, with users expressing interest in its integration capabilities, especially with Telegram and GitHub, while also noting some limitations and usability issues. There is a mix of enthusiasm for the innovation and skepticism about its complexity and long-term viability.

comments.author: ainch
comments.text: I was a little surprised to see a Telegram integration rather than Slack or Teams, given Anthropic's enterprise-first posture. But then I looked it up, and it turns out Telegram dwarfs both, at around 1bn MAUs, vs 50m and 300m respectively! I had no idea - reminds me of the time I found out Snapchat has 2x the userbase of Twitter.
comments.analysis: This comment provides a well-researched observation about user base sizes, adding valuable context to the choice of Telegram integration.

comments.author: mberg
comments.text: I just created agent-http that leverages the channels feature to enable you to wrap claude code with a http api. This provides an identical API to Agent API (https://github.com/coder/agentapi) that relies on terminal scraping to achieve this. Now you can interact with claude code in a headless manner using your subscription. Previously I think you had to do this via the Agents SDK which relies on api token use.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a practical implementation example that extends the discussed feature, showing a real-world application and technical insight.

comments.author: random17
comments.text:
I've been looking to build something similar to this so this is very timely!

What I wanted to build is a way for Claude Code to automatically receive reviews and CP failures from a Github PR and automatically revise code and respond to comments. It looks like with a custom Github PR channel I can get very close to this, although I do wish that a channel can be opened in a running session instead of having to create a new one. Hopefully they add that soon.
comments.analysis: This comment reflects a clear use case and constructive feedback on the feature's current limitations, indicating a good understanding of the product.

comments.author: vicchenai
comments.text: been running something similar with openclaw for a while now - github webhooks triggering code review, slack messages kicking off tasks, etc. nice to see anthropic building this natively into claude code. the telegram/discord support is a smart call too, way more devs hang out there than people realize.
comments.analysis: Provides relevant comparative experience and supports the value of native integration, enhancing the discussion with practical insight.

comments.author: wewewedxfgdf
comments.text:
I enabled the github connector in claude web interface.

I presumed Claude would then be able to clone repos, make commits, update the code in its container and then write it back to github.

Instead, the github connector does ..... nothing it all. It's very weird.
comments.analysis: This comment highlights a current limitation or bug, providing useful user feedback about the feature's functionality.

comments.author: dbbk
comments.text: It would have surely taken less time to just set up notifications for the Claude Code app? Are they ever going to do this? It's baffling to me that they're just skipping over letting you know when a task is completed... this is basic stuff.
comments.analysis: Expresses frustration about missing basic notification features, which is a valid critique but somewhat speculative about future plans.

comments.author: alexjurkiewicz
comments.text:
Claude is leaning into the idea of a local "session" being the host where everything connects.

I guess this makes sense for now. You can build integrations leveraging the user's personal access credentials. Later, once Claude takes over the world, they can move sessions to live in their own walled garden.
comments.analysis: Provides a thoughtful interpretation of the product's architecture and strategic direction, adding depth to the discussion.

comments.author: vrosas
comments.text: I also get the impression this is way more complicated than it needs to be. Or maybe it's simple and they keep inventing new terminology for stuff that basically already exists. The crypto bros did the same shit. Like, bidirectional communication has been a thing for decades. We're just changing what we call the client and the server? And the protocol is just strings the bot on the other end is a little better at reading?
comments.analysis: Offers a critical perspective on the complexity and terminology, which is a common concern but somewhat subjective.

comments.author: bronco21016
comments.text: Does anyone else have issues opening Claude.com domains on iOS? It’s infuriating I can never open documentation or the  usage page or account management portal on iOS on Safari. Works fine on a laptop. Mac, Windows, or Linux.
comments.analysis: Reports a specific technical issue with platform accessibility, useful for troubleshooting though limited in broader relevance.

comments.author: _pdp_
comments.text:
Very cool!

However, once remote capabilities are added to any software, it is virtually guaranteed that they will eventually be exploited as backdoors.

This means enterprise security solutions will need to develop the capability to distinguish between legitimate and illegitimate Claude Code instances.
comments.analysis: Raises a valid security concern about remote capabilities, adding an important dimension to the discussion.
